content.body: Semiconductors have electrical conductivity between conductors and insulators. Doping adds controlled impurities to modify conductivity. Key devices include diodes and transistors.

Diode:
Allows current in one direction only (forward bias)
Forward bias: p-side positive → conducts
Reverse bias: p-side negative → does not conduct

LED (Light Emitting Diode): emits light when forward biased

Transistor (NPN):
Base current controls collector-emitter current
Small base current → large collector current (amplification)
Current gain = Ic/Ib

content.title: Logic Gates
content.body:
AND gate: output HIGH only if ALL inputs HIGH
OR gate: output HIGH if ANY input HIGH
NOT gate: output is OPPOSITE of input
NAND = NOT + AND
NOR = NOT + OR

Truth table AND:
A=0,B=0 → 0
A=1,B=0 → 0
A=0,B=1 → 0
A=1,B=1 → 1

content.title: Applications
content.body:
Diode: rectification (AC→DC), protection circuits
Transistor: amplifiers, switches, digital circuits
Logic gates: computers, calculators, control systems
Microchips contain billions of transistors

questions.question: A diode conducts in
questions.explanation: Diode is a one-way valve — allows current only when forward biased (p-side positive)

questions.question: AND gate output is 1 only when
questions.explanation: AND gate: 1 output only if ALL inputs are 1

questions.question: NOT gate with input 0 gives output
questions.explanation: NOT gate inverts: NOT 0 = 1, NOT 1 = 0

questions.question: NPN transistor: small base current causes
questions.explanation: Transistor amplification: small base current controls large collector-emitter current

questions.question: OR gate output is 0 only when
questions.explanation: OR gate: output 1 if ANY input is 1; output 0 only if ALL inputs are 0

questions.question: LED differs from ordinary diode because
options: it emits light when forward biased
questions.answer: it emits light when forward biased
questions.explanation: LED: Light Emitting Diode — semiconductor junction emits photons when electrons recombine with holes

questions.question: NAND gate is equivalent to
questions.explanation: NAND = NOT-AND: opposite output of AND gate

questions.question: Transistor current gain = 100. Base current = 0.01mA. Collector current =
questions.explanation: Ic = gain × Ib = 100 × 0.01 = 1mA

questions.question: Binary logic 1 represents
questions.explanation: In digital electronics: logic 1 = high voltage (e.g. 5V or 3.3V), logic 0 = low voltage (~0V)

questions.question: Half-wave rectification uses how many diodes?
questions.explanation: Half-wave rectifier: one diode blocks the negative half of AC cycle, passing only positive half

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Diode: allows current in one direction only
Forward bias: current flows (anode + to cathode)
Reverse bias: no current

Half-wave rectification: uses 1 diode
Full-wave rectification: uses 4 diodes (bridge)

content.title: Diode Symbol
content.body:
Arrow points in direction of conventional current flow.
P-N junction diode made from semiconductor.
Forward voltage drop ≈ 0.6-0.7V for silicon diode
LEDs (Light Emitting Diodes) emit light when forward biased. Used in displays, indicators, and energy-efficient lighting.

questions.question: A diode allows current to flow
questions.explanation: Diode is a one-way valve for electric current.

questions.question: Forward bias means
questions.explanation: Forward bias: positive terminal to anode, negative to cathode - allows current.

questions.question: Half-wave rectification uses
questions.explanation: Half-wave rectifier uses a single diode, passing only positive half of AC cycle.

questions.question: Rectification converts
questions.explanation: Rectification converts alternating current to direct current using diodes.

questions.question: LED stands for
questions.explanation: LED = Light Emitting Diode - emits photons when current flows through it.

questions.question: Full-wave bridge rectifier uses
questions.explanation: Bridge rectifier uses 4 diodes arranged to use both halves of AC cycle.

questions.question: Output of half-wave rectifier is
questions.explanation: Half-wave rectifier gives pulsating DC - one-directional but not smooth.

questions.question: To smooth rectified DC we use
questions.explanation: Capacitor charges during peak and discharges between peaks, smoothing the output.

questions.question: Reverse bias diode
questions.explanation: Reverse biased diode blocks current except tiny leakage current.

questions.question: Zener diode is used for
questions.explanation: Zener diode maintains constant voltage across it - used in power supply regulation.

content.body:
Transistor as switch:
Base current controls collector current
Current gain (hFE) = Ic / Ib

Logic Gates:
AND: output high only if ALL inputs high
OR: output high if ANY input high
NOT: output is opposite of input

content.title: Transistor Switch
content.body:
Base current 0.01A, gain=100
Collector current = 100 × 0.01 = 1A
Small signal controls large current
Logic gates are the building blocks of digital electronics and computers. They use binary (0 and 1) signals. NAND and NOR are universal gates - any logic circuit can be made from them.

questions.question: Transistor has three terminals named
questions.explanation: Transistor terminals: Base (control input), Collector, Emitter.

questions.question: Current gain of transistor =
questions.explanation: Current gain hFE = collector current / base current.

questions.question: AND gate output is 1 when
questions.explanation: AND gate: output HIGH only when ALL inputs are HIGH.

questions.question: OR gate output is 0 when
questions.explanation: OR gate output is LOW only when ALL inputs are LOW.

questions.question: NOT gate with input 1 gives output
questions.explanation: NOT gate inverts the input: 1→0, 0→1.

questions.question: NAND gate is AND followed by
questions.explanation: NAND = NOT AND. Output is opposite of AND gate.

questions.question: In digital electronics, binary 1 represents
questions.explanation: Logic HIGH (1) is typically 3.3V-5V; Logic LOW (0) is ~0V.

questions.question: NOR gate output is 1 only when
questions.explanation: NOR = NOT OR. Output HIGH only when ALL inputs are LOW.

questions.question: Universal gate means
options: Any logic circuit can be built from it alone
questions.answer: Any logic circuit can be built from it alone
questions.explanation: NAND and NOR are universal - any boolean function can be implemented using only NANDs or only NORs.

questions.question: XOR gate output is 1 when
questions.explanation: XOR (exclusive OR): output HIGH when inputs are different.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

content.body: Electronic sensors convert physical quantities into electrical signals. Combined with transistors and logic gates, they form automatic control systems used in homes, industry, and medicine.

Common sensors:
LDR (Light Dependent Resistor): resistance ↓ in bright light
Thermistor (NTC): resistance ↓ as temperature rises
Microphone: sound → electrical signal
Strain gauge: measures force/deformation

content.title: Automatic Lighting Circuit
content.body:
LDR in voltage divider:
- Dark: LDR resistance high → voltage at base high → transistor ON → lamp ON
- Light: LDR resistance low → voltage at base low → transistor OFF → lamp OFF

questions.question: LDR resistance in bright light
questions.explanation: LDR = Light Dependent Resistor. Resistance decreases as light intensity increases.

questions.question: NTC thermistor resistance as temperature rises
questions.explanation: NTC = Negative Temperature Coefficient. Resistance decreases with temperature rise.

questions.question: In voltage divider with LDR and fixed resistor, output voltage depends on
questions.explanation: Voltage divider: Vout = Vsupply × R2/(R1+R2). Changing either resistance changes Vout.

questions.question: Transducer converts
options: One form of energy/signal to another
questions.answer: One form of energy/signal to another
questions.explanation: Transducers convert one quantity to another: LDR converts light to resistance change.

questions.question: Which sensor is used in a fire alarm
questions.explanation: Thermistor detects temperature rise; when it exceeds threshold, alarm triggers.

questions.question: A feedback control system
questions.explanation: Feedback: system measures output and adjusts input to maintain desired result.

questions.question: Thermostat is an example of
questions.explanation: Thermostat measures temperature (output) and switches heater on/off to maintain setpoint (feedback).

questions.question: Op-amp in comparator circuit
options: Compares two voltages and outputs high or low
questions.answer: Compares two voltages and outputs high or low
questions.explanation: Comparator: if V+ > V−, output HIGH; if V+ < V−, output LOW.

questions.question: Which sensor detects sound
questions.explanation: Microphone converts sound waves (pressure variations) into electrical signal.

questions.question: Relay is used to
options: Allow small current to control large current circuit
questions.answer: Allow small current to control large current circuit
questions.explanation: Relay: electromagnet switched by small transistor current opens/closes high-current switch.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Electronics. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Electronics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Electronics builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Electronics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Electronics. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Electronics include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Electronics. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
